metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and test the concrete idempotency mechanism for preventing duplicate backfill artifacts (e.g. 'before writing, check for an existing backfill record with source=backfill_classified for this bug id; skip if found'), and add an AC that asserts re-running the script on a bug that already has a backfill artifact produces no new record.
evidence: 'NOT a STATUS event — a separate backfill artifact' — since it is not folded into state, the filter 'state["close_class"] is ABSENT' remains satisfied after the first run.
AC #2 only asserts skipping bugs that already have state["close_class"] (authoritative value), NOT skipping bugs that already have a backfill artifact.
No AC or test fixture covers the case of re-running the script on a bug that already has a backfill artifact but still lacks state["close_class"].
findings.finding: The idempotency mechanism is unspecified and the stated filter is insufficient: backfill records are explicitly NOT STATUS events and NOT folded into state, so state["close_class"] remains absent after a backfill write, meaning a second script run would re-process and re-emit a duplicate backfill record for the same bug.
findings.location: Files section (scripts/backfill_bug_class.py — 'Idempotent (re-running does not duplicate)') / Acceptance Criteria AC #2
scenarios: Operator runs the script twice (e.g. after a crash mid-run); bugs already backfilled get a second duplicate record emitted.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: 'Before writing, the script queries existing backfill artifacts for the bug id; if one with source="backfill_classified" already exists, the bug is skipped.' Add AC: 'A bug with no state["close_class"] but an existing backfill artifact is SKIPPED on re-run (no duplicate emitted) — asserted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding test fixture) that verifies a bug already having a backfill artifact (source=="backfill_classified") is NOT re-emitted on a second run, covering the full idempotency guarantee stated in Files.
evidence: Files: 'Idempotent (re-running does not duplicate)'
AC 2: 'a bug that already has state["close_class"] is NOT overwritten/re-emitted (idempotence + authoritative-value protection) — asserted'

Backfill artifacts are 'NOT a STATUS event — a separate backfill artifact' and are not folded into compiled state, so state["close_class"] may still be ABSENT after backfill, meaning the skip-condition in AC 2 would not fire on re-run
findings.finding: The Files section declares the script 'Idempotent (re-running does not duplicate)' in the broad sense (any re-run), but AC 2 only verifies the narrower case where a bug already has state["close_class"] (i.e., was closed with --class). A bug that was backfilled on a first run now has a backfill artifact but may NOT have close_class in compiled state (backfill artifacts are explicitly NOT STATUS events and are segregated), so a second run could re-emit a duplicate backfill record. This broader idempotency behaviour is described but not verified.
findings.location: Files section ('Idempotent (re-running does not duplicate)') vs AC 2 ('a bug that already has state["close_class"] is NOT overwritten/re-emitted')
scenarios: Operator runs the script twice; the second run finds the same bugs with ABSENT state["close_class"] (because the backfill artifact is not in compiled state) and emits duplicate backfill records.
findings.suggested_fix: Track idempotency by checking for an existing backfill artifact (e.g., query the backfill store for records with source=="backfill_classified" for the given bug id) before writing, and add an AC: 'a bug that already has a backfill record with source=="backfill_classified" is skipped on re-run — asserted'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the existing storage primitive (event file, sidecar file, or a new JSON artifact alongside the ticket dir) the backfill record will use, cite its prior art in the codebase, and explain why a STATUS event was rejected in favor of this new artifact type.
evidence: Plan: 'write a labeled backfill record (NOT a STATUS event — a separate backfill artifact) carrying source="backfill_classified", confidence="classified"'
Existing close_class write path: `_processors.py:208-209` folds only from STATUS events with `close_class` key
MetricSpec/MetricValue in registry.py are compute-on-demand, not persisted records
No existing 'backfill artifact store' or non-event persistent record type found in codebase search
findings.finding: The plan introduces a 'separate backfill artifact (NOT a STATUS event)' as the write target for backfilled close_class values, but no such artifact type or storage mechanism exists in the codebase — there is no prior art for a non-event, non-MetricSpec persistent record in the rebar store. The plan does not cite or justify this novel pattern versus the established approach of writing a labeled STATUS event or a MetricSpec registry entry.
findings.location: Files section — scripts/backfill_bug_class.py description
scenarios: An executor must invent the storage format, file location, and read-back API for this artifact — the plan provides no prior art reference or schema for what a 'backfill artifact' looks like on disk.
The AC asserts `record.source == 'backfill_classified'` but 'record' has no defined type or storage location, making the AC unverifiable without implementation decisions the plan defers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding test fixture) that verifies a bug already holding a backfill artifact (`source=="backfill_classified"`) is NOT re-emitted on a second invocation, covering the full idempotency guarantee for the backfill-artifact path (not just the authoritative `state['close_class']` path).
evidence: Files section: 'Idempotent (re-running does not duplicate)'
AC-2: 'a bug that already has state["close_class"] is NOT overwritten/re-emitted (idempotence + authoritative-value protection)'
Files section: 'NOT a STATUS event — a separate backfill artifact' — backfill artifacts are not folded into compiled state by the reducer, so state['close_class'] remains ABSENT after backfilling.
findings.finding: The idempotency guarantee is incomplete: AC-2 only asserts that bugs already having `state['close_class']` (i.e., closed with an authoritative `--class`) are skipped, but backfill artifacts are explicitly NOT STATUS events and are NOT folded into compiled state — so after a first run a previously-backfilled bug still has ABSENT `state['close_class']`, meaning a second run would re-emit a duplicate backfill artifact.
findings.location: ## Files ('Idempotent (re-running does not duplicate)') vs Acceptance Criteria AC-2
scenarios: Operator runs the script, the script completes, then re-runs it (e.g. after a config change or to verify output). The second run finds the same bugs with ABSENT state['close_class'] and appends duplicate backfill records.
Partial run (interrupted halfway) is resumed — bugs classified in the first half are re-processed and receive a second backfill artifact.
findings.suggested_fix: In the idempotency check, look up existing backfill artifacts (e.g., scan the backfill store for records with `source=="backfill_classified"` keyed on bug-id) in addition to checking `state['close_class']`, and skip if a backfill record already exists. Add a corresponding AC: 'Running the script a second time on an already-backfilled bug produces no new records.'

findings.finding: The plan provides no validation mechanism on real data — only synthetic unit-test fixtures are listed in the ACs. There is no before/after count, run-report, or spot-check criterion verifying that the script produces sensible output when executed against the actual bug registry.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Running `python scripts/backfill_bug_class.py --registry <path>` on the staging registry exits 0 and prints a classification summary; a manual spot-check of at least one `regression` record confirms it traces to a REVERT event.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the idempotency mechanism in the plan: describe how `backfill_bug_class.py` detects a pre-existing backfill artifact for a bug (e.g., query existing artifacts with `source=backfill_classified` before writing, or rely on store upsert by bug_id+source key) so the implementation is unambiguous.
evidence: Plan asserts idempotency but gives no implementation detail: does it query existing backfill artifacts by bug_id+source? Check a flag in state? Rely on the store's upsert semantics?

The AC covers idempotence for bugs already carrying `close_class` in state, but the backfill artifact deduplication path (re-running the script after a partial run that already wrote some backfill records) is unspecified.
findings.finding: The idempotency mechanism is asserted ('re-running does not duplicate') but the detection method is unspecified — the plan does not describe how the script determines that a backfill record already exists for a given bug before skipping it.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_bug_class.py — Files section: 'Idempotent (re-running does not duplicate)'
scenarios: Script is run, writes 500 backfill records, then crashes. Re-run: if idempotency relies on `state['close_class']` absence but backfill records are NOT folded into state, the same 500 bugs are re-written, duplicating artifacts.
findings.suggested_fix: Before writing, query the artifact store for an existing record with `(bug_id, source='backfill_classified')`; if found, skip and increment `skipped_already_backfilled` counter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the access function (or specify a raw-scan pattern with explicit file suffix) for reading `gate_error_v1` records from the `COMPLETION_VERDICT` stream, so the script covers both plan-review and completion-gate errors.
evidence: gate_error_sidecar.py:31-35: _GATE_EVENT_TYPE maps 'completion' -> 'COMPLETION_VERDICT', so completion-gate errors ride COMPLETION_VERDICT not REVIEW_RESULT
plan_review/sidecar.py:164: all_review_results() reads only REVIEW_RESULT-suffix files
completion_sidecar.py: no all_completion_verdicts() or equivalent bulk reader exists
Plan says 'raw scan' without naming any access function or module for the COMPLETION_VERDICT half of the scan
findings.finding: The plan's `gate_error_v1` scan covers both the `REVIEW_RESULT` stream (plan/code-review gate errors) AND the `COMPLETION_VERDICT` stream (completion gate errors), per gate_error_sidecar.py:31-35. The plan names `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results()` for the BLOCK signal (REVIEW_RESULT stream) but for the `gate_error_v1` scan it only says 'raw scan' without naming any access function for the COMPLETION_VERDICT stream. `completion_sidecar.py` exposes only `latest_fail_verdict` and `latest_pass_record` (both single-record, schema-filtered readers) — no bulk `all_completion_verdicts()` analogue exists. An implementor following the plan will either silently miss completion-gate errors or must write undirected raw file-scan code with no specified symbol to call.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section: 'env_integration signal: a gate_error_v1 ERROR record on the bug's REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT streams (raw scan; schema gate_error_v1)'
scenarios: A closed bug whose env/integration failure happened at the completion gate (not plan-review gate) will have its gate_error_v1 record on COMPLETION_VERDICT only — the script as specified would miss it and fall through to 'undetermined'
Implementor writes the script using only all_review_results() and inadvertently covers only plan/code-review gate errors, silently missing the completion-gate signal
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Data-access section: 'For COMPLETION_VERDICT-stream gate errors, walk the ticket dir for files matching `*-COMPLETION_VERDICT.json`, load each, and check `data.get("schema") == "gate_error_v1"` — mirroring the REVIEW_RESULT scan. No bulk `all_completion_verdicts()` exists; raw scan is the correct approach for both streams.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the gate_error_v1 scan must cover BOTH stream types: add a note specifying that the script scans '-REVIEW_RESULT.json' files for plan_review/code_review gate errors AND '-COMPLETION_VERDICT.json' files for completion gate errors, and add a second env_integration fixture (or parameterise the existing one) that places the gate_error_v1 record on the COMPLETION_VERDICT stream.
evidence: gate_error_sidecar.py _GATE_EVENT_TYPE: {'plan_review': 'REVIEW_RESULT', 'code_review': 'REVIEW_RESULT', 'completion': 'COMPLETION_VERDICT'}
Plan says 'raw scan; schema gate_error_v1' but does not name an API or specify scanning both stream types
Test fixture for env_integration: 'a bug with a gate_error_v1 ERROR' — unspecified which stream the fixture writes to
gate_dispatch.py:677: emit_gate_error(ticket_id, 'completion', ...) writes to COMPLETION_VERDICT stream, not REVIEW_RESULT
findings.finding: The gate_error_v1 scan mechanism is incompletely specified: gate_error_v1 records are written to TWO distinct file streams — REVIEW_RESULT (for plan_review/code_review gates) AND COMPLETION_VERDICT (for the completion gate, per gate_error_sidecar.py's _GATE_EVENT_TYPE map). The plan says 'raw scan' of 'REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT streams' but does not specify that this requires two separate file-pattern scans ('-REVIEW_RESULT.json' and '-COMPLETION_VERDICT.json' suffix files), and the env_integration test fixture only crafts a REVIEW_RESULT-stream error. An executor implementing only a REVIEW_RESULT scan would silently miss completion-gate LLMUnavailableError records, producing false undetermined classifications for a real class of env_integration bugs.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section ('env_integration signal') and Files section ('gate_error_v1 ERROR on the bug's REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT streams')
scenarios: A bug closed after an LLMUnavailableError at the completion gate has gate_error_v1 on COMPLETION_VERDICT files only; a REVIEW_RESULT-only scan misclassifies it as undetermined.
The env_integration AC test passes because the test fixture writes to REVIEW_RESULT, but the implementation silently misses completion-gate errors in production.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Data-access section under 'env_integration signal', replace 'a gate_error_v1 ERROR record on the bug's REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT streams (raw scan; schema gate_error_v1)' with: 'a gate_error_v1 ERROR record on either the REVIEW_RESULT stream (plan_review/code_review gate errors; scan files ending -REVIEW_RESULT.json) OR the COMPLETION_VERDICT stream (completion gate errors; scan files ending -COMPLETION_VERDICT.json) — two separate raw file scans, filtering by schema=="gate_error_v1".' Add a second env_integration test fixture that places the record on COMPLETION_VERDICT to cover the completion-gate path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit signal-priority rule to the Approach section specifying which signal wins when a bug matches multiple (e.g., 'revert of STATUS/COMMITS → regression takes highest precedence; BLOCK → plan_defect next; gate_error_v1 → env_integration last; all others → undetermined') and add an AC assertion or test fixture for a bug carrying two signals to verify the rule is implemented correctly.
evidence: Plan lists three disjoint signal checks but states no ordering or priority when multiple fire
CLOSE_CLASSES is a single-valued field — only one class can be assigned per bug
Cached prior review finding f642207e99095b9c6f35 independently identifies the same gap
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a signal-priority (precedence) rule for the case where a single closed bug carries multiple signals simultaneously — e.g., both a STATUS-event revert (regression signal) AND a gate_error_v1 ERROR (env_integration signal). The executor must invent a tiebreak design decision that is silent in the spec.
findings.location: Approach section and Data-access ground truth section
scenarios: A bug whose fix was reverted (STATUS revert → regression) AND whose plan-review gate threw an LLMUnavailableError (gate_error_v1 → env_integration): the script must pick one but has no stated rule.
Two different executor implementations may produce different classifications for the same bug, making the backfill non-reproducible across re-runs on different code versions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit signal-priority ordering to the Approach section (e.g., `regression` > `plan_defect` > `env_integration`, checked in that order, first match wins) so the decision rule is co-located with the classification protocol.
evidence: Plan defines REVERT signal → `regression`, BLOCK signal → `plan_defect`, gate_error signal → `env_integration` as separate bullets with no 'else-if' or priority ordering between them.
Plan's Approach section: 'Classifying solely from REVERT / plan-review BLOCK / gate ERROR linkage' — lists them flat without ordering.
No AC fixture covers a bug with multiple signals simultaneously.
Prior plan-review cache finding at `.rebar/cache/plan-review/5062-30a8-9153-4c61/f642207e99095b9c6f35.json` independently identified this same gap.
findings.finding: The plan defines three exclusive output classes (`regression`, `plan_defect`, `env_integration`) driven by three independent structural signals, but specifies no precedence rule for the case where two or more signals are simultaneously present on the same closed bug. The decision protocol for signal priority is referenced implicitly (the signals are listed as if mutually exclusive) but never stated.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth — signal definitions / Approach section
scenarios: A bug was reverted (STATUS revert exists) AND had a plan-review BLOCK: executor must pick between `regression` and `plan_defect` with no guidance.
Two different executors implement different priority orderings, producing inconsistent backfill results across runs or environments.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'Signals are checked in priority order: (1) REVERT of STATUS/COMMITS → `regression`; (2) plan-review BLOCK → `plan_defect`; (3) gate_error_v1 ERROR → `env_integration`; (4) none → `undetermined`. First matching signal wins; remaining signals are recorded in the evidence payload but do not change the class.'

findings.finding: No precedence rule stated when a closed bug carries BOTH a REVERT signal and a BLOCK linkage — executor must invent this design choice.
findings.location: Approach section / classification logic
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'If both REVERT and BLOCK signals are present, REVERT takes precedence and the record is classified as regression.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/recovery path to the plan: either a `--purge` companion flag that removes all `source=="backfill_classified"` records for a given ticket (or all tickets), or a documented one-liner query using the `source` field, AND assert the post-rollback schema state (backfill artifacts removed, authoritative values untouched).
evidence: Files section specifies '--dry-run prints without writing' — a pre-run guard only, not a rollback path.

ABSENCE: No mention of how to identify and remove only backfill-sourced records, no --undo/--purge flag, no pre-run snapshot/export step, no description of what the store looks like after a partial or full rollback.
'Every backfilled value is labeled and segregated' — the source field exists but no query or command to exploit it for rollback is described.
findings.finding: The plan does not describe the persisted schema state AFTER rollback — the `--dry-run` flag prevents writes but there is no stated mechanism to purge or retire incorrectly-written backfill artifacts after a committed run. If the heuristic classification logic is found to be wrong post-run, there is no documented back-out path that restores the pre-backfill state.
findings.location: ## Files (scripts/backfill_bug_class.py) / ## Approach
scenarios: The REVERT-linkage heuristic mis-classifies a set of bugs as 'regression'; the operator wants to undo the backfill and re-run with corrected logic — there is no documented path to purge the existing backfill artifacts.
A partial run is interrupted after N bugs are written; the remaining bugs are unclassified. The plan has no stated procedure to bring the store back to a clean pre-backfill state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit priority order for signal evaluation (e.g. regression > plan_defect > env_integration > undetermined) in the plan so the executor has a deterministic tiebreak.
evidence: The plan lists three independent signals (REVERT, BLOCK, gate_error_v1) but nowhere states their evaluation order or priority when more than one fires on the same bug.
No AC fixture covers a multi-signal bug, so the tiebreak is neither specified nor tested.
findings.finding: When a bug carries multiple signals simultaneously (e.g. both a STATUS revert AND a gate_error_v1 ERROR), the plan does not specify which signal takes priority — an executor must choose the ordering arbitrarily, producing non-deterministic classification.
findings.location: Approach section / Files section (scripts/backfill_bug_class.py description)
scenarios: A bug that was reverted AND had a gate error could be classified as either 'regression' or 'env_integration' depending on which signal the executor checks first.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Approach section: 'Signals are evaluated in priority order: (1) REVERT of STATUS/COMMITS → regression; (2) REVIEW_RESULT BLOCK → plan_defect; (3) gate_error_v1 ERROR → env_integration; (4) undetermined. The first matching signal wins.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrency-safety mechanism for idempotence (e.g., a conditional write / CAS on the backfill artifact, a per-bug lock, or a store-level unique constraint) so that concurrent invocations cannot produce duplicate records for the same bug.
evidence: 'Idempotent (re-running does not duplicate)' — stated as a property but no enforcement mechanism is described
AC line: 'a bug that already has state["close_class"] is NOT overwritten/re-emitted (idempotence + authoritative-value protection) — asserted' — asserts the outcome but not the implementation
No mention of a lock, CAS, unique constraint, or transaction around the check-then-write sequence
findings.finding: The idempotence guarantee is stated as a goal ('re-running does not duplicate') but the mechanism that enforces it is never specified. Without a described atomic check-and-set, unique constraint, or CAS operation, concurrent invocations of the script follow a check-then-act pattern: both read `close_class` absent, both proceed to write, producing duplicate backfill records. The plan does not indicate whether the store supports transactions or a conditional-write primitive for this artifact type.
findings.location: Files section / Approach section — 'Idempotent (re-running does not duplicate)'
scenarios: A CI job or operator re-triggers the backfill script while a prior run is still in progress; both instances pass the 'close_class absent?' check simultaneously and both write a backfill record for the same bug, producing duplicates that the registry must later deduplicate or that silently inflate counts.
findings.suggested_fix: Document that the write uses a conditional 'insert-if-absent' primitive (or equivalent) against the store, and assert in the test that running the script twice serially produces exactly one backfill record per bug.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a batching/throttle strategy (e.g., a `--batch-size N` parameter with a configurable inter-batch sleep, defaulting to a small safe value) so the backfill does not hold the store write lock across all closed bugs in a single unthrottled transaction.
evidence: 'One-time backfill of close_class for already-closed bugs'
'iterate closed bugs lacking close_class, classify from the signals above, and write a labeled backfill record' — no batch size, throttle, or chunk strategy mentioned anywhere in the plan text.
ABSENCE: No --batch-size parameter, inter-batch sleep, or write-rate limit anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The backfill iterates all closed bugs in one unthrottled sweep with no batching, chunk size, or inter-batch sleep — for a large repo this becomes a single long-running pass that also invokes all_review_results() per bug, potentially holding store write locks for an extended period and starving concurrent normal writes.
findings.location: ## What / ## Approach / scripts/backfill_bug_class.py
scenarios: A repo with hundreds of historical closed bugs processes all of them in one blocking sweep; each bug calls all_review_results() (I/O) and potentially appends a write, holding the store write lock across the entire set.
Concurrent normal writes (transitions, comments) time out or fail during the long-running backfill run.
findings.suggested_fix: Accept `--batch-size` (default 25) and `--sleep-ms` (default 50) arguments; commit writes per batch and sleep between batches; document the recommended invocation for large stores in the plan's Approach section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify per-bug exception isolation in `backfill_bug_class.py`: wrap each bug's classify+write in a try/except, log failures with bug-id and error, continue to the next bug, and emit a final summary (processed count, skipped count, error count) so operators can confirm the run completed and audit any failures.
evidence: Plan says 'iterate closed bugs lacking close_class, classify from the signals above, and write a labeled backfill record' — no mention of per-iteration error handling.
ABSENCE: no retry, continue-on-error, or error-count/summary specification for the bulk loop.
'all_review_results(id, repo_root=)' is an external call that can fail; 'gate_error_v1' scan is file I/O — both are unguarded failure points inside the iteration loop.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify per-bug exception isolation: if `all_review_results()` or the gate_error_v1 scan raises an exception for a single bug during a bulk backfill run, the script's behavior (abort-all vs. log-and-continue) is unspecified.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_bug_class.py — Approach section / 'iterate closed bugs lacking close_class'
scenarios: One bug has a corrupted sidecar stream; `all_review_results()` raises — the script aborts, leaving the remaining bugs unprocessed with no indication of partial completion.
Re-run after partial failure re-processes already-done bugs (idempotency helps) but still fails at the same broken bug indefinitely unless skipped.
findings.suggested_fix: In the main loop: `for bug_id in candidates: try: classify_and_write(bug_id) except Exception as e: logger.error('backfill_failed', bug_id=bug_id, error=str(e)); error_count += 1; continue`. At completion: `logger.info('backfill_complete', processed=ok, errors=error_count, skipped=skipped)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add structured end-of-run summary logging to `backfill_bug_class.py`: emit counts of bugs processed, skipped (already had close_class), and classified into each class (`regression`, `plan_defect`, `env_integration`, `undetermined`, `error`) so operators can sanity-check signal coverage.
evidence: ABSENCE: no mention of structured logging, progress indicators, or end-of-run statistics.
The plan mentions 'logged' only for the retention-cap pruning case ('a very old BLOCK may be pruned — an accepted best-effort limit, logged'), not for the overall run.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability instrumentation (progress logging, per-signal hit counts, final summary) for the backfill run itself — operators running this against a large bug population cannot tell whether it completed correctly, how many bugs were classified into each class, or whether the signals fired as expected.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_bug_class.py — Files / Approach sections
scenarios: Backfill runs to completion but the `regression` signal never fired (perhaps due to a bug in the target_event_type filter) — without per-class counts, the operator has no way to detect this.
A deployment environment has all sidecar streams unavailable — every bug gets classified `undetermined` with no signal, and without summary stats this goes unnoticed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add non-happy-path behavior to the spec: define the error handling strategy for signal-read failures (skip-and-log vs. abort), write failures (rollback vs. continue), and document the script's exit code and end-of-run summary so operators can verify completeness.
evidence: '--dry-run prints without writing' — only happy-path variant named
'Retention caps at 50/ticket, so a very old BLOCK may be pruned — an accepted best-effort limit, logged' — logging acknowledged but failure mode of the surrounding call is not described
No AC line addresses what the script does when a signal-read call throws or a write fails
No mention of exit code, partial-run resumability, or error summary at end of run
findings.finding: The CLI script's non-happy-path behavior is entirely unspecified: what happens when `all_review_results()` raises (e.g., sidecar store inaccessible or pruned beyond the stated 50-record cap), when the bug store write fails mid-batch, or when the iterator over closed bugs encounters a corrupt/unreadable ticket. The plan only describes `--dry-run` (a happy-path variant) and the success outcome; no error state, partial-batch recovery, or exit-code contract is defined.
findings.location: Files section / Tests section / Acceptance Criteria — script behavior under errors omitted throughout
scenarios: An operator runs the backfill against a large corpus; `all_review_results()` throws a network/IO error on ticket #500 of 2000; with no specified error handling the script may silently skip the ticket, abort the whole run, or crash mid-write, leaving the operator with no clear indication of which bugs were processed and which were not.

A write failure mid-batch leaves some bugs backfilled and others not, with no documented recovery path; re-running may or may not be safe depending on idempotence (itself underspecified per the T9 finding above).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and specify the actual read path for `gate_error_v1` detection: `all_review_results()` schema-guards these records out; the script needs a separate raw file scan (e.g. iterating ticket dir files ending in `-REVIEW_RESULT.json` and `-COMPLETION_VERDICT.json`, parsing each, and checking `data.get('schema') == 'gate_error_v1'`) — name this mechanism explicitly in the plan rather than relying on `all_review_results()`.
evidence: Plan states: 'a gate_error_v1 ERROR record on the bug's REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT streams (raw scan; schema gate_error_v1)'
sidecar.py:164-210 `all_review_results` schema-guards: `payload.get('schema') in ('plan_review_result_v1', 'plan_review_result_v2')` — gate_error_v1 payloads are skipped silently
sidecar.py:185 only lists files ending in f'-{EVENT_TYPE}.json' where EVENT_TYPE='REVIEW_RESULT' — COMPLETION_VERDICT-hosted gate_error_v1 records are never scanned
gate_error_sidecar.py:31-35: _GATE_EVENT_TYPE maps 'completion' to 'COMPLETION_VERDICT', confirming gate_error_v1 records can appear on a stream `all_review_results` never touches
No existing public function to scan gate_error_v1 records off the COMPLETION_VERDICT stream was found in the codebase
findings.finding: `all_review_results()` schema-guards its output to `plan_review_result_v1`/`_v2` only and will silently drop every `gate_error_v1` record; the plan's assertion that the `env_integration` signal can be detected via a raw scan of those sidecar streams is ungrounded as a single read path, and no existing function reads `gate_error_v1` records off the `COMPLETION_VERDICT` stream.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section — 'env_integration signal' paragraph; also 'plan-review BLOCK signal' paragraph naming `all_review_results`
scenarios: A bug closed after a completion-gate infrastructure outage writes its gate_error_v1 onto COMPLETION_VERDICT, not REVIEW_RESULT; the script reads REVIEW_RESULT via all_review_results(), finds nothing, and classifies the bug as 'undetermined' instead of 'env_integration'.
Even for plan-review-gate outages (gate_error_v1 on REVIEW_RESULT), all_review_results() schema-guards them out, so the raw scan the plan alludes to is never actually performed by the named API.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the env_integration signal description with: 'raw file scan of the ticket dir for files ending in -REVIEW_RESULT.json and -COMPLETION_VERDICT.json; for each, load and check data.get("schema") == "gate_error_v1" — do NOT use all_review_results() for this, as it schema-guards gate_error_v1 records out.' Alternatively, reference `rebar.llm.gate_error_sidecar.GATE_ERROR_SCHEMA` as the schema constant to match against.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by code inspection) that the `gate_error_v1` scan does NOT use `all_review_results()` but instead uses a raw file scan or a dedicated reader that accepts the `gate_error_v1` schema; name the specific function/approach in the plan.
evidence: Plan: 'a gate_error_v1 ERROR record on the bug's REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT streams (raw scan; schema gate_error_v1)'
sidecar.py:198-200: `payload.get('schema') in ('plan_review_result_v1', 'plan_review_result_v2')` — schema guard explicitly excludes gate_error_v1
gate_error_sidecar.py:31-34 confirms gate_error_v1 records ride on REVIEW_RESULT, but all_review_results() skips them
findings.finding: The plan asserts `all_review_results()` can scan for `gate_error_v1` ERROR records on the REVIEW_RESULT stream, but the actual implementation of `all_review_results()` in `sidecar.py` is schema-guarded to only accept `plan_review_result_v1` and `plan_review_result_v2` — a `gate_error_v1` record on the same stream would be silently skipped. The plan's capability assertion ('raw scan; schema `gate_error_v1`') is unverified against the real API.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth — env_integration signal bullet
scenarios: The env_integration classification never fires in practice because all_review_results() silently drops the gate_error_v1 payloads it was intended to surface — all gate-errored bugs fall through to `undetermined`.
The AC fixture for env_integration passes in tests (using a crafted raw-scan) but the real script uses the wrong API.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the vague 'raw scan' phrase with an explicit reference to scanning sidecar files directly (e.g., iterating the REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT event files and filtering by `payload.get('schema') == 'gate_error_v1'`), or add a new `gate_error_results(ticket_id)` helper to `gate_error_sidecar.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `all_review_results()` reference for the `env_integration` signal with a concrete raw-scan specification (e.g., reference `gate_error_sidecar`'s `_scan()` pattern or a named helper) that reads both the `REVIEW_RESULT` and `COMPLETION_VERDICT` streams and filters `schema == 'gate_error_v1'`.
evidence: Plan: 'read them via `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results(id, repo_root=)` (returns retained payloads; check `verdict=="BLOCK"`)'
Plan: 'a `gate_error_v1` ERROR record on the bug's `REVIEW_RESULT`/`COMPLETION_VERDICT` streams (raw scan; schema `gate_error_v1`)'

sidecar.py:198-201: `if isinstance(payload, dict) and payload.get('schema') in ('plan_review_result_v1', 'plan_review_result_v2'):` — gate_error_v1 payloads fail this guard and are never appended to `out`
The BLOCK-signal description also conflates the same `all_review_results()` call with the separate gate_error scan, creating ambiguity about which call each signal uses
findings.finding: `all_review_results()` schema-guards its return value to `plan_review_result_v1`/`plan_review_result_v2` only (verified at `sidecar.py:198-201`), so it will silently drop every `gate_error_v1` record. The plan says the `env_integration` signal comes from a `gate_error_v1` ERROR record scanned via this API, which is structurally impossible as written.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth — env_integration signal / Files section (`scripts/backfill_bug_class.py`)
scenarios: An executor implements the env_integration signal by calling all_review_results() as instructed — the scan returns zero gate_error_v1 records for every bug, classifying all env/integration bugs as `undetermined`.

An executor notices the conflict and invents their own raw-scan helper — but without a specified stream suffix or payload-extraction contract, their implementation may differ from the one tested in the heldout test.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Data-access section, replace 'read them via `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results()`' for the gate_error signal with: 'raw-scan both the `REVIEW_RESULT` and `COMPLETION_VERDICT` sidecar directories (suffix-based, mirroring `gate_error_sidecar._GATE_EVENT_TYPE`), parse each `event["data"]`, and filter for `payload.get("schema") == "gate_error_v1"`.' Keep the `all_review_results()` reference only for the BLOCK signal.

findings.finding: No acceptance criterion or step verifies that the backfill script was actually executed against real closed-bug data — the ACs only prove classification logic is correct in a temp store, leaving a gap between 'script is correct' and 'backfill is done'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section / end-state
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] `python scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` executes against the live store, exits 0, and prints a summary of records written; a subsequent registry query for closed bugs returns at least one record with source=="backfill_classified" and confidence=="classified".'

findings.finding: The test file path 'tests/unit/scripts/test_backfill_bug_class.py' rests on a false premise: the directory tests/unit/scripts/ does not exist in the repository. Script tests live at tests/scripts/, not under tests/unit/. If an executor creates the file at the plan-specified path without creating the directory, the test will not be discovered by the existing conftest/pytest infrastructure.
findings.location: Files section / AC line 'tests/unit/scripts/test_backfill_bug_class.py passes'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the planned test path to `tests/scripts/test_backfill_bug_class.py`, consistent with where script tests already live (tests/scripts/test_autodeploy_materialize_probe.py, etc.).

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a bug linked to a plan-review BLOCK' is a detectable structural signal, but no mechanism in the codebase surfaces a plan-review BLOCK verdict into compiled ticket state. The REVIEW_RESULT sidecar (which records verdicts) is explicitly reducer-ignored and absent from reduce_ticket() output. Detecting this signal requires scanning raw REVIEW_RESULT sidecar files in each dep-target's ticket directory — a non-trivial I/O operation the plan neither names nor specifies.
findings.location: Approach section / 'a bug linked to a plan-review BLOCK -> plan_defect'

findings.finding: The plan places the new test at tests/unit/scripts/test_backfill_bug_class.py, but the directory tests/unit/scripts/ does not exist in the repository and the plan does not mention creating it or any __init__.py needed for pytest discovery under that path.
findings.location: Files section: tests/unit/scripts/test_backfill_bug_class.py
findings.suggested_fix: Either move the test to tests/unit/test_backfill_bug_class.py (matching the convention of test_check_build_env_locked.py and test_release_guards.py), or explicitly add tests/unit/scripts/__init__.py to the Files list.

findings.finding: The REVERT signal is under-specified: state['reverts'] accumulates ALL REVERT events (including reverts of COMMENTs, LINKs, ARCHIVED events, etc.) — a non-empty reverts list on a closed bug does NOT reliably indicate 'closed via a revert'. The script needs a qualified filter (e.g., any revert record whose target_event_type is STATUS, or whose timestamp precedes the close event) to make this signal meaningful, but the plan specifies none.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a bug closed via a REVERT -> regression'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'A bug counts as closed-via-REVERT only when state["reverts"] contains at least one record with target_event_type == "STATUS" whose timestamp is at or near the close edge — i.e., a STATUS event was reverted on this ticket.'

findings.finding: The BLOCK-linkage mechanism for `plan_defect` is unimplementable from compiled state alone: `state['deps']` stores dependency target IDs and relations but carries no verdict field; REVIEW_RESULT sidecars (which hold plan-review BLOCK verdicts) are explicitly reducer-IGNORED and never enter compiled state. Detecting 'a bug linked to a plan-review BLOCK' requires resolving each dep target's directory, reading its REVIEW_RESULT sidecar files, and checking their verdict — a multi-step, sidecar-aware lookup the plan does not describe at all.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a bug linked to a plan-review BLOCK -> plan_defect'

findings.finding: The plan's key signal — 'a bug linked to a plan-review BLOCK' — conflates two structurally distinct data sources without specifying which one the script will read: (1) a REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event (reducer-ignored, not in compiled state) carrying verdict=='BLOCK', or (2) a ticket-level blocking-relation link (`deps[].relation in {'blocks','depends_on'}`). These require different access paths and produce different classifications. The plan never resolves this ambiguity.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a bug linked to a plan-review BLOCK -> plan_defect'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'BLOCK signal: read the ticket's REVIEW_RESULT sidecar files via `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.read_all_sidecars(tracker_dir, ticket_id)` and check if any retained sidecar has `verdict=="BLOCK"`. Distinguish from generic blocking deps (deps[].relation) which are NOT used for plan_defect classification.'

findings.finding: The 'plan-review BLOCK' detection rule references an undefined data access path: REVIEW_RESULT events are reducer-ignored (`src/rebar/reducer/_version.py:77` places REVIEW_RESULT in `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES`), so compiled ticket state never surfaces plan-review BLOCK verdicts. The plan names this signal as a classification trigger but never specifies whether the script reads (a) the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar files directly, (b) ticket deps/links, or (c) some other source — all of which require different access paths and produce different classifications.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a bug linked to a plan-review BLOCK -> plan_defect'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'BLOCK detection reads REVIEW_RESULT sidecar JSON files from `.rebar/cache/plan-review/<bug-id>/` (using `scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py` as a reference for enumeration); a bug is BLOCK-linked if any sidecar payload has `verdict == "BLOCK"`.' This avoids relying on compiled state, which never contains REVIEW_RESULT data.

findings.finding: The REVERT classification rule has an ambiguous match predicate: `state['reverts']` in compiled state accumulates ALL REVERT events (reverts of COMMENTs, LINKs, ARCHIVED events, etc.) — not only reverts of STATUS/close events. The plan says 'a bug closed via a REVERT → regression' but gives no protocol for which reverts qualify, leaving the executor to invent a filter predicate.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a bug closed via a REVERT -> regression'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'A bug counts as closed-via-REVERT only when `state["reverts"]` contains at least one record whose `target_event_type == "STATUS"` (a status-event revert); reverts of COMMENTs, LINKs, and ARCHIVED events are excluded from this signal.'

findings.finding: The backfill script writes to the bug registry (a one-time, potentially irreversible operation), but the plan provides no rollback path, dry-run mode, or pre-write backup mechanism. Per T4, acknowledgement of irreversibility alone is insufficient — a concrete reversibility mechanism is required.
findings.location: Approach section / Files: scripts/backfill_bug_class.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--dry-run` flag to `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` that prints the would-be writes without committing them; document a pre-run export step (e.g., `cp bug_registry.json bug_registry.json.bak`) in the plan's Approach section as the rollback path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete function or access pattern for scanning gate_error_v1 ERROR records (analogous to how `all_review_results` is named for the BLOCK signal).
evidence: REVERT signal: 'reduced state["reverts"]' — concrete field named.
BLOCK signal: 'read them via rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results(id, repo_root=)' — concrete function named.
gate_error_v1 signal: 'a gate_error_v1 ERROR record on the bug's REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT streams (raw scan; schema gate_error_v1)' — no function or access pattern named.
findings.finding: The access method for scanning gate_error_v1 ERROR records is described only as 'raw scan' without naming a helper function or API, while the BLOCK signal names a concrete function (`all_review_results`). An executor implementing the gate_error_v1 check lacks a concrete starting point.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section — env_integration signal; Files section (scripts/backfill_bug_class.py)
scenarios: Executor searches the codebase for a gate_error_v1 scan helper and picks the wrong one, or implements a bespoke scan that misses records.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `gate_error_v1` detection requires a RAW file scan (not `all_review_results()` which schema-guards them out), and point to `gate_error_sidecar.GATE_ERROR_SCHEMA` + the file-listing pattern in `completion_sidecar.py` or consider adding a narrow `all_gate_errors(ticket_id)` reader to `gate_error_sidecar.py`.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:198-201: `payload.get('schema') in ('plan_review_result_v1', 'plan_review_result_v2')` — gate_error_v1 is explicitly excluded
src/rebar/llm/gate_error_sidecar.py:31-35: gate_error_v1 records ride the REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT streams
Plan phrase: 'raw scan' — correctly implies a separate walk, but does not warn that `all_review_results()` cannot be reused, risking duplicate directory-walk code
No existing `all_gate_errors(ticket_id)` reader in gate_error_sidecar.py — the script will need to write one or inline the walk
findings.finding: `all_review_results()` (from `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar`) schema-guards to `plan_review_result_v1/v2` and silently skips `gate_error_v1` records, yet the `env_integration` signal requires finding those exact records in the same `REVIEW_RESULT` stream. An implementer who calls `all_review_results()` for gate-error detection gets zero hits. The plan says 'raw scan' for this case, but does not make explicit that the named function is inapplicable, risking a re-implementation of the same directory-walk boilerplate.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section — 'env_integration signal: a gate_error_v1 ERROR record on the bug's REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT streams (raw scan; schema gate_error_v1)'
scenarios: Implementer calls `all_review_results(ticket_id)` to search for gate_error_v1 records, gets [], classifies all affected bugs as `undetermined` instead of `env_integration`.
Implementer correctly inlines a raw directory walk, but duplicates the file-listing/json-loading pattern already in `all_review_results` and `plan_review_impact_distribution.py`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-liner note in the Data-access section: '`all_review_results()` schema-guards to plan_review_result_v1/v2 — use a raw directory walk filtered to `schema==gate_error_v1` (see `gate_error_sidecar.GATE_ERROR_SCHEMA`), mirroring the pattern in `completion_sidecar.latest_fail_verdict`.' Optionally add `all_gate_errors(ticket_id)` to `gate_error_sidecar.py` to avoid inlining the walk in the script.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
